system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — complete-fingerstyle-jazz-guitar-book

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This book teaches solo fingerstyle jazz guitar from first principles through a carefully sequenced three-section arc. Section One (Chapters 1–4) establishes physical mechanics and foundational notation: posture, hand geometry, stroke types, rhythmic vocabulary, and the earliest two-voice combinations of melody and bass. Section Two (Chapters 5–8) moves into harmonic craft, teaching the oom-pah bass pattern, thirds, tenths, and sixths as successive harmonization tools that progressively fill out the texture from a single melody line toward a three-voice arrangement. Section Three (Chapters 9–15) addresses the mature concerns of the jazz guitarist: harmonic theory, self-accompaniment devices, accompanist technique, expressive jazz language, cycle-of-fifths voice leading, structural conventions (intros, endings, turnarounds, tags, modulations), and finally a set of complete pieces that synthesize all prior learning. The book moves from the hand to the page to the harmony to the idiom, treating each layer as a prerequisite for the next.

## Physical Mechanics and Foundational Technique

Chapter 1 establishes the physical substrate that everything else depends on. The guitar is held by right elbow, left leg, and chest, freeing the fretting hand entirely. The right hand operates with the thumb in an inverted hitchhiking position and the first three fingers tilted back toward the bridge—a geometry that prevents collisions and enables clean stroke mechanics. The book assigns precise names to the fingers (p-i-m-a-c) and establishes two stroke types with strict domain separation: the rest stroke, in which the finger moves through the string and comes to rest on the adjacent string, is the primary melody-production technique; the free stroke, in which the finger clears all adjacent strings, is used by the thumb at all times (never rest stroke) and by melody fingers on adjacent-string situations where rest stroke would cause cancellation (Chapter 3). The alternating i-m pattern is the foundational fingerstyle locomotion principle—described explicitly as analogous to walking—and the one-finger-per-string scheme is prescribed as an ergonomic alternative for repeated patterns. The book forbids pre-resting plucking fingers on strings before striking, mandates metronome use within 60–100 bpm with accuracy prioritized over speed, and requires open strings to be damped by light touch.

## Two-Voice Technique and Rhythmic Framework

Chapters 2 through 4 establish the rhythmic and notational framework within which all arrangements operate. The book defines jazz's structural architecture as a quarter-note pulse supporting eighth-note melodies, with the swing or shuffle feel—long-short interpretation of eighth notes based on triplet subdivision—applied strategically at slower to medium tempos. Dotted notes extend duration by half their value, and the book specifies that ties (not dots) must be used when extending notes across measure lines; 4/4 measures are notated as 2/4+2/4 for readability. The stem-direction convention maps note stems to hand assignment (stems up = melody/right hand; stems down = bass/left hand, by implication). Counting aloud is prescribed as a self-monitoring tool for rhythmic accuracy. Chapter 4 introduces rhythmic counterpoint as an arranging principle: busy melodies pair with longer bass notes, and sparse melodies pair with more active bass lines.

## Harmonic Building Blocks and the Arranging System

The book's central harmonic vocabulary is built interval by interval. Chapter 5 introduces the chromatic scale as a universal half-step measuring tool and defines major thirds as four half steps and minor thirds as three, establishing the stacking-thirds formula—major third plus fifth equals major chord, minor third plus fifth equals minor chord—as the procedural explanation of triads. The root/fifth bass pattern (oom-pah) is the foundational accompaniment structure, with altered fifths handled by sharpening or flattening as required and mixed-rhythm bass used for variation. The key arranging rule from Chapter 5 is: when the melody contains a major or minor third, the bass uses root or fifth; when the melody lacks a third, the bass supplies it to harmonically identify the chord.

Chapters 6–8 teach a progressive hierarchy of harmonization intervals. Thirds below (Chapter 6) require free stroke on adjacent strings and are evaluated against the underlying chord via chord-tone matching, with non-chord tones acceptable when they pass an ear test; arpeggiation is the default texture but abandoned when melody leaps unpredictably. Tenths (Chapter 7)—an octave-displaced third functioning as a bass note—are treated with independent bass rhythm rather than parallel rhythmic doubling, preventing monotony; daily scale-in-thirds practice with deliberate rhythmic variations is prescribed for developing facility. Sixths below (Chapter 8) function identically to thirds and tenths in their chord-tone relationships (melody root yields sixth-below third; melody third yields sixth-below fifth; melody fifth yields sixth-below seventh or ninth), but carry the added rule that the key note must stay on top to preserve major tonality. The more-is-less principle introduced in Chapter 8 characterizes the book's mature aesthetic stance: slow-moving bass, call-and-answer sequences, and selective harmonic devices create shape without clutter.

## Harmonic Theory and Voice Leading

Chapter 9 reframes the guitar as polyphonic and replaces static barre chords with partial chord fragments built from independent voices. The book defines three primary harmonic movement types: diatonic harmony (movement within the scale), the cycle of fifths (descending by perfect fifths, with V7–I resolution as the paradigm case), and chromatic root movement (progression by half steps). Seventh chords are established as the minimum harmonic unit of jazz harmony. A characteristic pedagogical move is the bidirectional transfer principle: exercises are converted into specific musical contexts (intros, endings, fills), and specific ideas from tunes are generalized through harmonic permutations across keys.

Chapter 13 deepens voice-leading instruction through cycle-of-fifths practice, identifying tritone pull (the unresolved Bb and E being pulled toward A and F in V7–I resolution) as the mechanism of harmonic tension, and the spread texture—bass alternating up a fourth and down a fifth, creating a tenth from the melody above—as an idiomatic arrangement texture. The book insists on the conceptual shift from chords as static formations to harmonic movement as several simultaneously moving voices, demonstrated through voice-by-voice analytical breakdowns.

## Self-Accompaniment Devices and the Accompanist's Role

Chapter 10 catalogs the full toolkit of self-accompaniment strategies in ascending complexity: root-movement bass (simplest), doubled bass notes (swing-era style), root/fifth alternation (folk and country idiom), and walking bass with chromatic approach notes (jazz idiom). Anticipation and delay devices vary rhythmic feel; arpeggiation serves slower tunes; sparse two-note inner-voice voicing produces full arrangements from minimal voice count. Moving tenths, drawn from Fats Waller and Art Tatum's stride piano practice, and rootless close voicing using fourths and tritones (effective in ensemble contexts where a bassist supplies the root) extend the toolkit. The chapter's governing principle is that musical effect and coherence matter more than strict interval patterns.

Chapter 11 distinguishes accompanist technique from solo playing by freeing the guitarist from the melody line. Comping—evolved from stride piano's sparse punctuation—is the core vocabulary. The comping density principle requires playing more in melodic holes and less when the soloist is active. In ensemble settings, the book prescribes voicing high on the top four or five strings when an electric bassist is present, and adopts the bass-first conception rule: think bass-plus-chord rather than chord-plus-bass to maximize harmonic flexibility.

## Jazz Expression and Song Structure

Chapter 12 defines the expressive devices of the jazz idiom: off-beat accents as a trademark phrasing device; ghosted notes de-emphasized through light picking or articulated via hammer-ons and pull-offs; slides and fall-offs produced by maintaining finger pressure while sliding, with long slides called glissandos; and rhythmic displacement—anticipation and delay reinterpreting written notes—as a defining jazz trademark. The book treats swing feel as notated straight but performed with triplet-based syncopation, and emphasizes that mastery requires learning jazz's street language through active listening to live and recorded music.

Chapter 14 teaches the structural conventions of jazz performance. Intros must end on V7 or a V7 substitute; the practical shortcut is reusing the song's final measures. Endings use V7–I, IV–I, or II–V7–I progressions. Turnarounds are functionally two-measure intros, using V7 to set up successive choruses. Tags (codas) are triggered by replacing the final I chord with V7. For modulations, the book establishes three methods with increasing sophistication: the two-beat V7–I substitution, back-cycling through the cycle of fifths, and the II–V7–I progression as the most idiomatic jazz bridge between keys. The II–V7 unit is identified as the core harmonic vocabulary for connecting song sections.

## The Synthetic Method and Aesthetic Principles

Chapter 15 demonstrates the book's complete method in nine finished arrangements, making the master's synthesizing approach explicit. The walkdown—a descending bass line with a common tone over a chord stack—is identified as a primary structural device. Specific harmonic techniques include descending chromatic key changes, half-step approach chords, diatonic cycle-of-fifths movement, deceptive cadences, and the tritone accompaniment technique. Rhythmic sophistication is demonstrated through triplet feel, double-time feel within ballads, and polymetric 3-on-4 phrasing. The method for converting lead-sheet melodies into solo arrangements—single-note melody plus chord symbols as the complete source material—is confirmed as the book's operative procedure throughout.

The book's animating aesthetic principle, stated explicitly in Chapter 15 and reinforced through Chapters 6, 8, and 10, is that whatever sounds good to the ear will work, constrained only by community musical standards. This ear-first pragmatism runs through every technical prescription: chord-tone matching is a test to pass, not a rule to follow mechanically; arpeggiation is abandoned when fingering demands it; voice-leading collisions can be valid arrangement solutions. The book teaches a craft in which physical mechanics, harmonic knowledge, and idiomatic listening are integrated into a single judgment—what the master calls, in different chapters, effect, impression, and ultimately the jazz sound itself.

# Chapter 3 — Section One/Part 3: Rhythming Around

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the core two-voice fingerstyle technique of combining rest stroke on the melody (played with i, m, or a) and simultaneous free stroke on the thumb for accompaniment, with the critical rule that adjacent strings require two free strokes to avoid cancellation. The chapter introduces the standard pattern-playing finger-to-string assignment (a→1st, m→2nd, i→3rd, p→4th/5th/6th) and establishes that bass rhythm functions as both a structural and expressive element—the constant bass as a drumlike accompaniment can shift the mood or character of a piece depending on whether quarter notes or half notes are used, a principle that elevates bass-rhythm choice from mechanical drill to arranging decision. Key arranging norms include the expectation that melody notes typically appear more frequently than bass notes in a solo guitar arrangement, the recognition that a lead sheet (single-note melody plus chord symbols) contains all the information needed to create a fingerstyle jazz arrangement, and practical guidance on tune selection (avoiding blistering tempos, complex syncopation, and unfriendly keys) and on detecting modulations through accidentals in melody and chords rather than explicit key-signature changes.

# Chapter 13 — Section Three/Part 5: Pedaling the Cycle of Fifths

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Chapter 13 teaches cycle-of-fifths practice as a foundational technique for building familiarity with all twelve keys and smooth harmonic movement, or voice leading. The chapter contrasts root-position playing (which works but sounds clunky) with voice-leading approaches that move voices by half-step, whole-step, or mixed intervals, emphasizing the core conceptual shift from thinking of chords as static dots to thinking of harmonic movement as several moving voices. Key techniques include contrary motion for V7-I progressions, understanding tritone pull (the unresolved Bb and E being pulled toward A and F in resolution), and the spread texture, where bass notes alternate up a fourth and down a fifth to create a tenth interval from the melody above. The master's characteristic moves visible here are the voice-by-voice breakdowns (such as the C7-to-F9 analysis) that reveal how each voice contributes to smooth, airy harmonic movement rather than parallel root motion. Load-bearing terminology introduced includes voice leading, harmonic rhythm, tritone pull, and spread as a fingerstyle arrangement texture.

# Chapter 14 — Section Three/Part 6: Intros, Endings, Turnarounds, Tags & Modulations

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch14.md`._

Chapter 14 teaches the harmonic architecture of jazz song structures: intros must end on V7 or a V7 substitute to lead into the tune, while endings employ V7-I, IV-I, or II-V7-I progressions, with the practical shortcut of reusing the song's final measures as an intro. The master reveals characteristic techniques like chromatic motion, cycle-of-fifths movement, whole-tone color, and low pedal tones in intro construction, and introduces the vamp as a repeated setup device requiring active listening to the soloist's cue. Turnarounds are functionally equivalent to two-measure intros, using the same V7-gateway principle to set up each successive chorus, while tags (codas) are triggered by the cue Tag! and initiated by replacing the final I chord with V7. For modulations, the chapter establishes three methods with increasing sophistication: the two-beat V7-I substitution for instant key changes, back-cycling through the cycle of fifths, and the II-V7-I progression as the most idiomatic jazz bridge between keys. Load-bearing terms include vamp, turnaround, tag, modulation, and the II-V7 unit as the core harmonic vocabulary for connecting song sections.

# Chapter 15 — Section Three/Part 7: Fun Jazz

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch15.md`._

Chapter 15 teaches fingerstyle jazz arrangement through nine pieces, grounded in the aesthetic principle of implying harmonic richness with sparse voicings while prioritizing melody. The author introduces key harmonic devices—descending chromatic key changes, half-step approach chords, diatonic cycle-of-fifths movement, deceptive cadences, and the tritone accompaniment technique—alongside specific rhythmic concepts like triplet feel, double-time feel within ballads, and polymetric 3-on-4 phrasing. A signature technique throughout is the walkdown, defined as a descending bass line with a common tone over a chord stack, which serves as the structural hook in several pieces. The chapter also teaches a transferable method for converting single-line melodies into self-accompanied fingerstyle arrangements, and demonstrates how sustained eighth-note melodic flow (attributed to Joe Pass) maintains rhythmic momentum despite sparse bass notes. Throughout, the author's characteristic move is stylistic fusion—layering bebop vocabulary over stride piano imitation, for instance—balanced by an overarching principle that whatever sounds good to the ear will work, tempered only by community musical standards.

## Guitar body centering posture

> The guitar is held in place by the right
> elbow, left leg, and the chest. You should
> not have to hold it in place with the left hand.
> The classical guitar position, with the left
> foot raised on a footstool or high stool rung,
> is a good model of fingerstyle guitar posture.

**Significance (p. 10).** Establishes the foundational posture principle that the fretting hand must remain free for technique, not for supporting the instrument.

## Finger naming convention

> The traditional symbols for the right hand
>
> fingers are:
>
> Thumb p
>
> Index i
>
> Middle m
>
> Ring a
>
> Pinkie c

**Significance (p. 10).** Defines the standard p-i-m-a notation system used throughout the entire method, making this the key to reading every subsequent exercise.

## Right hand position setup

> The thumb p of the right hand points out toward
> the left in an inverted hitch-hiking position. This
> creates space between the thumb and index finger
> so that they won't run into each other.
>
> Tilt the first three fingers back (toward the bridge)
> so that they are in line when you place them all ona
> single string.

**Significance (p. 11).** Specifies the exact right-hand geometry that prevents thumb-finger collisions and aligns the plucking fingers for efficient stroke mechanics.

## Rest stroke technique

> To emphasize a melody, use a volume producing technique called the rest stroke. To play a rest
> stroke, place your guitar, arm, and hand in the position described above. Temporarily rest the thumb
> lightly on the sixth string away from the other fingers. Hover the index i, middle m, and ring finger a,
> directly in front of the first string. Keeping the finger supple and not frozen, pluck the first string with
> the left side of the fingertip and nail — if you have nails — and come to rest on the adjacent second
> string.

**Significance (p. 11).** Defines the rest stroke — the primary melody-production technique of the entire method — with precise finger placement and stroke direction.

## Striking while in motion rule

> When you pluck, do not rest the plucking finger on the string to be plucked. Instead, momentarily
> hover the finger in front of the string to be plucked. Start swinging the finger before you meet the
> string to be struck, just as you would in baseball, tennis, or handball.

**Significance (p. 11).** States the critical rule against pre-resting on strings, explaining why a moving stroke is necessary to avoid damping ringing notes.

## Alternating fingers i-m

> Right-hand stroking with alternating iand m fingers (i-m) is the way to produce a fluent flow
> of notes. This alternating finger technique is like walking by putting one foot in front of the other.
> Using just one plucking finger would be like trying to walk by hopping on one foot.

**Significance (p. 12).** Introduces the i-m alternation as the foundational fingerstyle locomotion principle, analogizing it to walking to make the concept intuitive.

## Left hand thumb position

> The ball of the left thumb should be well
> under the neck of the instrument, working in
> opposition to the fingers on the fretboard. In
> some instances the thumb is used to play
> bass notes on the sixth string, but it should
> not be hooked around the neck in anticipa-
> tion of these movements, as it will inhibit
> other more prevalent movements and
> stretches.

**Significance (p. 12).** Defines the correct left-thumb placement and explicitly forbids wraparound thumb, which would restrict the stretches required for jazz chord voicings.

## One finger per string scheme

> When notes occur in a repeated pattern across the same set of strings, right hand fingers may
> be assigned one finger per string to match this pattern. This is particularly true in three finger/three
> string combinations
>
> In the next exercise the a finger takes care of the first string, m plays the second string, and /
>
> plays the third.

**Significance (p. 17).** Introduces the second major right-hand picking scheme — finger-per-string assignment — as the ergonomic alternative to fixed alternation when strings repeat in a pattern.

## Thumb free stroke technique

> Hover the thumb above the sixth string. Move it in a
> down and forward movement , striking the sixth string with the lower, outer edge of the thumb tip and
> nail. Clear the fifth string by arcing upward in a circular movement and returning to the starting
> position.

**Significance (p. 18).** Describes the specific thumb stroke geometry — down-forward strike followed by an upward arc — that distinguishes the free stroke from a rest stroke.

## Thumb does not use rest stroke

> Generally the thumb does not use the rest stroke as it can easily
> overpower the melody being played with the i, m, or a fingers.

**Significance (p. 18).** States a governing rule that keeps the bass thumb subordinate to the melody fingers, defining the volume balance central to fingerstyle jazz texture.

## Damping open strings

> If a sounding open string needs to stop at a specific moment,
> as when it is followed by a rest, damp it by touching it very lightly with a free finger. For now, start
> with any free right-hand finger.

**Significance (p. 26).** Introduces open-string damping as a distinct technical requirement because, unlike fretted notes, open strings cannot be silenced by releasing left-hand pressure.

## E minor pentatonic blues scale

> Here is the complete blues scale in an ascending and descending pattern. The scale begins
> and ends on E eight lines or spaces above. In this exercise, the scale returns to where it started.
> This distance is an octave. Memorize this scale. It’s called the E minor pentatonic blues scale.

**Significance (p. 24).** Names and defines the E minor pentatonic blues scale as the foundational melodic vocabulary for the jazz blues context being built throughout Section One.

## Two-part notation / stems direction

> In simple fingerstyle guitar notation, notes with stems pointed up are usually played with the /, m,
> or a fingers. For now, lightly rest your right hand thumb (p) on the sixth string to help you maintain
> correct hand position while using your /-m fingers to play the following exercise.

**Significance (p. 15).** Establishes the stem-direction convention that maps notation to hand assignment throughout the book, making it essential for reading all two-part arrangements.

## Tie symbol definition

> Atie symbol joins notes of the same
> pitch. The note on the left continues sounding for
> the duration of the note on the right. Do not pluck
> the second note. The combination of two tied
> quarter notes, below, sustains for a total of two
> beats. Notes can be tied within a measure or
> between measures.

**Significance (p. 29).** Defines the tie as distinct from a slur, specifying the no-repluck rule critical to reading sustain correctly in two-part solo jazz arrangements.

## Metronome use and tempo range

> To work on rhythmics you will need something to use as a reference beat. Without a constant
> reminder, there is a tendency to speed up when playing is easy, and slow down when playing is
> difficult. If you don’t have one, now is the time to get a metronome (or electronic drum machine).
> For starters, set the metronome within the range of a comfortable pace between 60-100 beats
> (metronome ticks) per minute. You want accuracy, not speed.

**Significance (p. 34).** Prescribes the metronome as a non-optional tool and gives a specific starting tempo range, framing accuracy over speed as the governing practice principle.

## Dotted Note Extension Rule

> A dot after a note extends the note for precisely half as long as the note itself. In 4/4
> time a half note normally receives two beats. If it were dotted, it would receive three beats, as in the
> example below.

**Significance (p. 36).** Defines foundational rule for dotted notes that governs all duration calculations.

## Dot vs Tie Distinction

> Although dotted notes and ties are both used as note extenders, there is a difference. A dot
> after a note always extends the note by half the original value of the note. A note with a tie attached
> to the proceeding note on the right extends the original note by the value of the second note. When
> music is notated, the choice between using dotted notes over tied notes is determined by the ease
> of readability.

**Significance (p. 36).** Distinguishes two easily-confused notation tools and gives rule for choosing between them.

## Two-Part Rhythm Technique

> RH: i-m or m-a, rest stroke; p, free stroke

**Significance (p. 37).** Specifies the right-hand technique combining melodic and bass voices, a core fingerstyle coordination pattern.

## Eighth Note Notation

> Individual eighth notes have a head, stem, and a single flag. Note how the eighth note rest differs
> from the quarter note rest.

**Significance (p. 39).** Introduces the visual anatomy of eighth notes essential to reading jazz melodies.

## Jazz Pulse and Melody Relationship

> The basic underlying pulse of jazz is a quarter note, on top of which is a melody based on a steady
> pulse of eighth notes.

**Significance (p. 39).** Defines the structural two-layer rhythmic architecture of jazz central to the entire method.

## Counting Eighth Notes

> In 4/4 time, or any meter in which a
> quarter note receives one beat (X/4), an
> eighth note or eighth rest receives a half
> beat. By convention, an eighth note falling
>
> ona beat is counted with 1, 2, 3 or 4, and an
>
> eighth note falling in between or offthe beat
> is counted with the word and.
>
> Eighth notes

**Significance (p. 40).** Establishes the 1-and-2-and counting system underpinning all subsequent exercises.

## Self-Monitoring While Counting

> Try counting the eighth-note subdivisions aloud, especially in trouble spots. If you listen to
> yourself as you count, you will most likely spot any inaccuracies. For instance, if you listen and
> find that at a certain point you are changing the speed of your counting to match what you think
> the music is asking you to play, stop and check. The pace or tempo of the underlying basic beat
> should always be the same, of course. Pencil in the counting if necessary.

**Significance (p. 40).** Diagnostic technique for self-correcting rhythmic errors.

## Swing/Shuffle Feel Definition

> But an authentic idiomatic interpretation would require eighth notes on 1, 2, 3 and 4 to be
> slightly longer in duration, and notes on and to be proportionally shorter. In early jazz, an accent
> was placed on 1 and 3. Later the place of emphasis changed, landing on 2 and 4. For now, stay
> with 1 and 3. This long-short feeling is called shuffle, or swing. It is also the feeling underlying the
> songs of rhythm and blues. The eighth-note feel will not show up in notes with a greater duration
> than eighth notes.

**Significance (p. 42).** Defines swing/shuffle, the defining rhythmic characteristic of jazz guitar.

## Swing Tempo Limitation

> This shuffle rhythm works best for slower-to-medium blues and jazz. When the tempo is very
> fast, play eighth notes more evenly.

**Significance (p. 42).** Prevents misapplying swing feel at fast tempos, linking rhythm interpretation to musical context.

## 4/4 Readability as 2/4+2/4

> Clear writing of music notation in a measure of 4/4 is organized rhythmically with 2/4 + 2/4 in mind.
> Look at the two lines of music below. They are rhythmically the same. Each measure of the top line
> is configured as though it were two half measures of two beats each. The second line is not incor-
> rect, but it is slightly more difficult to read. Keep this in mind when you begin writing your own music
> in Section Two of this

**Significance (p. 46).** Compositional/notation rule about thinking in half-measures for readability.

## Dotted Quarter Note Value

> In 4/4 time, a dotted half note receives three beats. Likewise, a
>
> dotted quarter note extends by a half beat, receiving a total of a beat and a half.

**Significance (p. 46).** Extends dotted-note rule to common quarter-note level.

## Tie vs Dot Across Measures

> Both ties and dotted notes extend the duration of notes, and in most cases are interchangeable,
> except across measures, where ties must be used.

**Significance (p. 46).** States the hard rule distinguishing when a tie is mandatory rather than optional.

## simultaneous rest/free stroke technique

> Continue to play the melody with the i, m, or a finger. To keep the melody projecting over the
> accompaniment, use the rest stroke. Simultaneously use a free stroke with the thumb. Combining
> two familiar techniques probably feels awkward at first. Don’t be surprised if you find yourself
> trying to play both thumb and fingers with the same kind of stroke (rest or free). Squeeze the
> strings toward each other with relaxed fingers working in a sideways motion across the plane of
> the strings. Don’t rip up and outward, which would make the strings “splang” against the fretboard.
> Make sure you keep your right hand in the inverted hitch-hiking position, with thumb well out, to
> avoid colliding with a finger.

**Significance (p. 51).** Core two-voice technique instruction for the chapter.

## adjacent strings: free stroke rule

> *When you play two notes on adjacent strings you have to use two free strokes. Otherwise, if you use a rest stroke on the higher string, it cancels out the lower string with the follow through. To make the free stroke louder, pluck harder.

**Significance (p. 52).** Specific technical rule that overrides default rest-stroke when adjacent strings involved.

## pattern playing finger-to-string assignment

> a plays 1st string
> m plays 2nd string
> i plays 3rd string
> p plays 4th, 5th, and 6th strings

**Significance (p. 52).** Standard fingerstyle pattern-playing hand assignment.

## constant bass as drum function

> The constant bass can be a driving force in simple blues. Use four quarter notes per measure. In this style the bass functions as a constant drum beat.

**Significance (p. 53).** Frames rhythmic role of bass voice as stylistic choice.

## bass accompaniment rhythm as mood/character

> Using a constant quarter-note bass isn’t necessarily the best way. It’s just another way. Play
> the same blues section with a constant half-note bass. The original Twofers and the altered
> version are both legitimate variations. Changing the bass accompaniment rhythm can change the
> mood or character of a piece. When blues or jazz musicians approach an existing melody or an
> improvisation, the nature of the underlying rhythm is often the first decision made.

**Significance (p. 56).** Elevates bass-rhythm choice from drill to arranging principle.

## melody vs bass note density norm

> In a typical solo guitar arrangement the number of melody notes per measure will generally be
> more frequent than the notes of the accompaniment (bass). This is the case in the example above.
> The reverse is less frequent, but it does occur: a slower melody with a faster bass movement.

**Significance (p. 57).** Structural norm for solo guitar arranging.

## lead sheet requirements for arranging

> The simplest musical notation of a tune is a lead sheet, which has a single-note melody with
> chord symbols above. This is all that is required to create a solo guitar arrangement.

**Significance (p. 60).** Defines minimum input needed to begin a fingerstyle jazz arrangement.

## tune selection: tempo/key/difficulty check

> Unless your technique is advanced, avoid choosing tunes that
> must be played at a blistering tempo, or with highly syncopated rhythms, or ones that are way up the
> neck and in an unfriendly key. If a melody is too difficult to play as a single line melody, adding
> harmony and bass lines won't make it any easier!

**Significance (p. 61).** Rule-set for candidate tune selection.

## detecting key changes in lead sheets

> in popular sheet music temporary key changes are not usually indicated by a
> new key signature. You need to examine the piece for clues, such as the simultaneous occurrence of
> accidentals in both the melody and chord symbols. If the melody notes and chords do not belong to
> the starting key, the tune has temporarily shifted to another key.

**Significance (p. 61).** Analytical method for identifying modulations from a lead sheet.

## Good guitar keys rule

> the most
> common solo guitar keys are C, G, D, A, and F major; A, E, B, and D minor. These keys provide
> plenty of opportunity to use the open strings, which makes the physical task of playing easier.

**Significance (p. 63).** Establishes foundational principle for key selection in fingerstyle arranging.

## Melody range minimum threshold

> tunes with a preponderance of notes lower
>
> than D, fourth string, are too low for two-part (melody and accompaniment) arranging.

**Significance (p. 63).** Concrete lower-bound rule for melody range viability.

## Brightness range rule

> if most of the notes are pitched below the second
> string, B, the arrangement will probably not sound very bright and won’t sparkle.

**Significance (p. 63).** Secondary tonal-quality criterion for register choice.

## Transposing accidentals

> When transposing to a new key, accidentals, sharps, flats, and naturals, not occurring in the
> original key must be transposed too. In measure three of Tune 2, there occurs a G sharp, not
> naturally found in the key of C. To move this accidental to the key of D, count five scale steps up (or
> use the trace down a column method) in D to A and make it sharp.

> When transposing a melody, the chord symbols must be transposed too. Chord letter names (A,
> B, C, etc.) are transposed exactly the same as notes, by using the counting steps or column tracing
> method. The chord qualities (maj7, m7, etc.) stay the same.

**Significance (p. 67).** Rule that chord roots transpose but qualities do not.

## Notation convention stems up

> The stems of the melody notes point up making room for an accompanying part below.

**Significance (p. 68).** Core notational convention for two-voice fingerstyle.

## Beaming eighth notes for instruments

> Eighth notes and notes of smaller values in the vocal melody part are separated, and not
> beamed. This is so the singer can easily match the notes with syllables and words. However, for
> instrumentalists it is easier to read flagged notes (eighths, sixteenths, etc.) if they are beamed

**Significance (p. 68).** Practical reading-convention difference affecting source interpretation.

## Bass note from chord root

> The root of a chord is
> the important note for now. If the chord symbol is C, the root is C. For starters, just use the bass note
> with the same letter name as the chord name.

**Significance (p. 70).** Foundational rule for thumb-bass accompaniment.

## Bass rhythm vs melody density rule

> If the rhythm of the melody is busy and many-noted, you can
> complement it by playing longer notes in the bass. When the melody is made up of longer note

**Significance (p. 74).** Rhythmic counterpoint guideline balancing melody and bass.

> You can even place a repeated note in
> alternating lower and higher octaves. You will find this in traditional ragtime and folk blues styles

> Alternating octaves work best

**Significance (p. 74).** Performance-readiness principle for fluent execution.

> oom-pah rhythm mixed with walking tenths. When rhythm sections evolved further, pianists
> played with a sparse left hand, punctuating much as a drummer might. This technique specifically
> came to be called comping.

**Significance (p. 135).** Etymology and historical evolution of comping.

## Comping density rule

> For starters, one rule of thumb is to play more when the soloist is not playing, and play less
> when he is. Play more when there are “holes’ to be filled, and don’t step all over the soloist when
> he is playing busily.

**Significance (p. 135).** Fundamental density rule for comping behind a soloist.

## Electric guitar/bass register separation

> Playing two electric instruments, such as electric guitar and electric bass, needs special care.
> If you play low-pitched, walking four-to-the-bar chords and your partner plays a walking bass line,
> it results in a muddy tangle of sounds in both texture and pitch. The solution is simple: play your
> thythm part, whether constant or comping, higher on the board on the top four or at most five
> strings.

**Significance (p. 135).** Actionable rule for voicing register alongside electric bass.

## Accompanying singers follow the leader

> When following the singer, follow the singer—unless the singer wants you to lead. It’s a bit like
> dancing together. Work with your singer. She has her own concerns. She probably doesn’t care
> how hip you are. She measures your worth as a function of how easy you make it for her to get
> in and out of the tune.

**Significance (p. 131).** Role-based rule for singer accompaniment.

## Single-line accompaniment

> As guitarists we tend to think that playing accompaniment necessarily means playing chords
> in some shape or form. What if you were a bass player or a saxophone player? To them,
> accompaniment means creating lines, melodic and rhythmic. “But guitarists can play chords.”
> Yes, but they don’t have to. For variety, try accompanying with a single line.

**Significance (p. 139).** Single-line accompaniment as deliberate stylistic option.

## Single-line backup Dixieland listening tip

> Tip: If you are having trouble with inventing a single-line backup, listen to the lines of New
> Orleans, early Chicago, or Dixieland jazz. Listen to how the clarinet, trombone, cornet, sax,
> drums, tuba and banjo work together. The horns are playing ensemble with single lines, every line
> complementing the total piece, with no train wrecks.

**Significance (p. 139).** Specific listening source and what to extract from it.

## Bass-first conception rule

> Think bass-plus-chord, not chord-plus-bass. There’s a difference. It’s best to conceive of a
> bass line first—planned or improvised—and then maybe add a chord, and not to conceive of
> chords first and then scout around for a bass line. If first you commit your fingers to a chord and
> afterward look for a bass line, the choice is much more limited.

**Significance (p. 141).** Generative priority rule for walking-bass-plus-chord technique.

## Walking bass study advice

> Also, many guitarists attempting to play walking-bass-line-with-chord comps play like a guitarist,
> not a bass player. Study bass? It wouldn't hurt. At least, to get it right, find transcribed bass lines
> and study them. Better yet, transcribe them yourself. Add chords later. Try to get the variety that
> bass players use. Bigger intervals, broken time, and textural changes are some of their
> techniques.

**Significance (p. 141).** Concrete remediation path for weak walking bass.

## Avoid constant bass doubling

> Today the bass doesn’t often play four quarter notes to the bar, especially during the
> first and last chorus. Older-style bass playing included much doubling of two same notes per
> measure. Unless you, as the guitarist/bassist, want to recreate vintage jazz, avoid constant
> doubling.

**Significance (p. 141).** Style-specific rule distinguishing modern from vintage bass walking.

## Guitar duet register division

> If you are working with another
> guitarist, you can profit by having a simple agreement on what is going to happen when and where.
> You might divide your guitars in half (drop that saw). Both can take turns staying at the other end
> of the range, for instance.

**Significance (p. 137).** Primary structural solution to guitar-duet register collision.

## Guitar duet alternate tuning

> Another way of keeping out of each other’s way is to tune your guitars differently. The late Carl
> Kress, accompanying George Barnes, used a guitar tuned a fifth lower. Later, Bucky Pizzarelli
> with a seven-string guitar (an extra-low A) backed George, who played a guitar tuned a fourth
> higher.

**Significance (p. 137).** Alternate tuning as second register-separation strategy with historical precedent.

## Walking-bass-plus-chord structure

> The constant rhythmic pulse of bass-plus-chord is a close cousin to the traditional flat-pick
> style of four-beats-to-the-bar. In bass-plus-chord, you have offset the lowest note of a three- or
> four-note chord, with the rest of the chord trailing by a quarter or eighth note.

**Significance (p. 141).** Defines mechanical rhythmic structure of walking-bass-plus-chord.

# Chapter 13 — Section Three/Part 5: Pedaling the Cycle of Fifths

_Pages 155-159._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. ## Why the Cycle Matters

> Jazz musicians practice phrases, licks, and routines using the cycle, as it is called. One reason
> is that playing a chord or melodic phrase through the cycle includes moving through all twelve
> keys, a good technique builder on any instrument. Practicing the cycle also familiarizes you with
> the sound of smooth harmonic movement, or voice leading.

**Significance (p. 155).** Dual pedagogical purpose of cycle practice.

## Root-in-Bass vs Voice Leading

> Always playing chords with the root
> in the bass works, but may not be as smooth or interesting as moving by half-step or whole-step
> intervals or mixed movement.

**Significance (p. 155).** Central stylistic rule privileging voice leading over root-position.

## Notation Convention Key of C

> Rather than writing key
> cancellations and new key signatures every time the chord changes in the cycle (sometimes twice
> per measure), | have used the convention of writing the whole cycle as though it were in the key
> of C. Accidentals are notated.

**Significance (p. 155).** Notational shorthand used throughout cycle exercises.

## Harmonic Rhythm and Root Position

> By examination you can see that the harmony changes every two beats. As you move through
> the exercise, the last note of the first group of four eighth notes moves to either five scale steps
> down or four scale steps up. The pattern has the root as the first note in every case.

**Significance (p. 155).** Exact intervallic mechanism of root-movement phrases.

## V7-I Opposing Bass Movement

> Accompany the top line with a simple root bass note for the next V7-| movements. As the top
> note descends by half step the root note has its own opposing pattern, not parallel to the top.

**Significance (p. 156).** Contrary motion as voice-leading technique for V7-I.

## V7-V7 Mixed Movement Analysis

> Each measure has the same pattern, but within the measure there are two different harmonic
> movements. The simple C triad and the following B’ notes together make up C7. The following F9
> has the G note in common with C. The middle voice moves by half step from E to E>. The bottom line
> travels from C, the root, to A, the third of F9, by BY, the '7 of the C7 note. The final lower line move is
> to F, the root, which sets up the shift to the B’7, the root of the next part in the cycle. The total effect
> is a smooth move through continual dominant sounds.

**Significance (p. 156).** Voice-by-voice breakdown of dominant-to-dominant motion.

## Tritone Pull Explanation

> The move from C7 to F in the example below is a classic harmonic move. You can just feel the
> tug of the unresolved B’ and E (the tritone interval of a’5) being pulled by the A and F in the resolved
> F chord, can’t you?

**Significance (p. 156).** Names tritone-resolution mechanism underlying dominant function.

## Harmonic Movement as Moving Voices

> If you think of harmonic movement as several moving voices rather than a glob of dots on a chord
> box grid, your arrangements and improvisations will be more airy, more interesting and less clunky.

**Significance (p. 156).** Core conceptual shift from chord-block thinking to linear voice-leading.

## The Spread Bass-Melody Tenth

> The root bass notes alternate in moving up a fourth and then down a fifth. Each new bass note is
> a tenth interval from the melody note above. The bass moves from low to high. This makes a good
> spread, sounding truly like a bass accompaniment to a melody high above.

**Significance (p. 157).** Defines 'spread' texture as fingerstyle arrangement technique.

## Bassman vs The Spread

> This melody and The spread, two examples above, are very similar. The distinguishing feature is
>
> the movement of the lowest line.

**Significance (p. 158).** Focus on bass-line motion as differentiating variable.

# Chapter 14 — Section Three/Part 6: Intros, Endings, Turnarounds, Tags & Modulations

_Pages 160-167._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. ## Intro last chord rule

> Intros can start on any chord. The last chord of the intro must be a V7 or V7 substitute in order to
> move into the first chord of the tune itself. Endings typically use V7-I or substitute. IV-l is also used,
> especially in soul or gospel music.

**Significance (p. 160).** This is the foundational harmonic rule governing how intros and endings must be constructed, establishing the V7 gateway principle that drives all subsequent examples.

## Last measures as intro

> To make a point of intro ordinariness, the last four- or eight measures of the tune itself can often
> be used, including the melody.

**Significance (p. 160).** This practical shortcut teaches players how to instantly generate idiomatic intros from material they already know, reducing the technique to a reuse of existing structure.

## Intro harmonic movement example

> This intro starts solidly in F but immediately moves out of key for a little surprise. The first
> harmonic movement is chromatic, F to E79. The next moves cyclically to Am7>5, chromatically
> to Ab7 and Gm7, cyclically to C7 and stays on the final V7 with a little sprinkle of whole-tone scale
> thrown in at the C+7.
> Finally, the low pedal tone C is a common device, used also in the example above. It says,
> “Here comes the melody.

**Significance (p. 160).** This annotated example explicitly names the compositional techniques at work (chromatic motion, cycle motion, whole-tone color, low pedal tone), teaching how to construct a harmonically varied intro step by step.

## Vamp definition and function

> A vamp is an intro that is repeated over and over until a cue is given to move into the song
> proper. It occurs in instrumental jazz, but more often is used with singers. Besides setting up the
> feeling and tempo of the tune, the guitarist is repeatedly offering the singer a chance to launch her
> song. It's definitely an ears-up situation.

**Significance (p. 161).** This defines the vamp as a distinct formal device with a specific interactive function, alerting the reader to the listening responsibility it demands.

## II-V7 within one measure

> Especially in jazz, the V7 chord is proceeded by a II chord, as it is here in the last measure. You
> can spot one in measure two, also.

**Significance (p. 161).** This reinforces the II-V7 as the core jazz harmonic unit and trains the reader to recognize it embedded within intro examples.

## 2-in-1 intro/ending trick

> There are three measures leading to a | chord in the last measure, followed by a V7, the classic
> setup chord. What if you just didn’t play the G13 on the third beat? Your intro would become an
> instant ending. It’s no trick, it’s just so. You've just multiplied your list of intros and endings by two.
>
> Another tip: Four measure intros are often two two measure units, so usually whatever you use
> for the last two measures can stand alone as a two measure intro.

**Significance (p. 163).** This gives two concrete reuse rules — omit the final V7 to convert an intro to an ending, and truncate any four-bar intro to a two-bar intro — that dramatically multiply a player's practical vocabulary.

## Endings harmonic formula

> Endings end, and usually on a | chord. V7-I, IV-1, and especially li-V7-| are standard.

**Significance (p. 163).** This concisely states the three harmonic formulas for endings, giving the player a ranked hierarchy to apply in performance.

## Turnaround definition and function

> In most jazz situations, tunes will have several choruses. Typically, the last two measures of
> the tune will set up the first measure of each following chorus with a turnaround. This is practically
> another intro, and whatever you would use as a two-measure intro of a tune you could use as a
> turnaround. Like introductions, turnarounds often start on a | chord and finish on a V7 or V7
> substitute chord.

**Significance (p. 164).** This formally defines the turnaround, links it structurally to the intro, and states its harmonic shape, establishing the conceptual equivalence that lets players share material across both contexts.

## Turnaround backing a singer

> About the only restriction is when you are backing a singer who is holding a note through the last
> two measures. You should then still be accompanying intelligently and not kicking out the jams.

**Significance (p. 164).** This is the one explicit rule limiting turnaround freedom, teaching situational restraint when the soloist's held melody supersedes harmonic improvisation.

## Turnaround with modulation rule

> If the first chord of the second chorus is other than a | chord, the two turnaround measures must
> modulate to the chord of the first measure.

**Significance (p. 164).** This states a critical structural rule that extends turnaround technique to modulating situations, a common real-world performance demand.

## Tag (coda) definition

> A tag, or coda, is a musical extension of the last out chorus of a tune. For one reason or another
> the band leader (or you alone) decides to stretch the music. A standard cue is usually given — “Tag!”
> At this point the replacement of the usual final | chord—E, here—with a V7 (or other substitute) chord
> is required. You have to listen up to hear what kind and how long of a tag it will be.

**Significance (p. 164).** This defines the tag, names its performance cue, and identifies the essential harmonic substitution (I -> V7) that initiates it, giving the player both the conceptual framework and the mechanical trigger.

## Instant modulation: V7-I

> How fast do you want to go? You can modulate as fast as two beats. Presume you have ended
> a tune on the standard | chord and have used two beats doing it. You are moving to D’. Use the
> last two beats to borrow V7 (A>7) from the upcoming new key, Db.
>
> You could even modulate just with one beat on A’7, but you would then arrive at the new key
> a beat too early.

**Significance (p. 165).** This teaches the minimum-time modulation technique, giving a precise beat-count rule and warning about the one-beat pitfall — essential practical knowledge for real-time key changes.

## Back-cycling modulation method

> Using the cycle of fifths as a method of movement, work backwards in the cycle to F7 and then
> C7. Viola! as my fiddle teacher used to say.
> From this chord outline you can create a realization that will fit the style and situation at hand.

**Significance (p. 166).** This teaches back-cycling as a systematic method for filling the harmonic gap between two keys during a modulation, directly applying the cycle-of-fifths concept to a compositional task.

## II-V7-I modulation method

> Again, we know that this one measure modulation begins with a | chord in C and ends with a
>
> setup V7 type chord of the new key, D. What to do in-between? You know from your other experi-
>
> ences that the most common jazz progression unit is Il-V7-I. Use it here then, by putting a I! of the
>
> upcoming new key in front of the new key V-7.
> This II-V7-I is part of the cycle.

**Significance (p. 166).** This teaches the II-V7-I substitution as the smoothest and most idiomatic method for bridging two keys, explicitly connecting it to the cycle and positioning it as the preferred jazz-language solution.

> The key changes from C to D moving chromatically by half step. Don’t let the chord quality get in
